text: The surgeon performs lateral dissection of the colon, aiming to improve visualization of the entire colon to identify any external features consistent with cancer.
The surgeon performs lateral mobilization of the colon, manipulating the tissue to improve visualization while awaiting the arrival of the colonoscope.

The surgeon visually examines the colon, which exhibits severe diverticulosis, noting the absence of previous diverticulitis episodes. The condition of the bowel raises concerns about the feasibility of creating an anastomosis.
The surgeon performs an intraoperative colonoscopy, inserting the colonoscope into the colon to visually inspect the bowel for lesions.
During the intraoperative colonoscopy, large stool balls are observed falling into the lumen of the colon, amidst the presence of severe diverticulosis.
The surgeon identifies a lesion located at thirty-five centimeters during the intraoperative colonoscopy.
The surgeon advances the colonoscope up to the splenic flexure, approximately seventy centimeters from the anal verge, during the intraoperative colonoscopy.
The surgeon carefully withdraws the colonoscope during the intraoperative colonoscopy, ensuring a clear view of the colon lumen.
The endoscopic camera view is adjusted to show the extent of the scope's reach within the colon, highlighting the progression of the scope during the procedure.
